reviewer.displayName: VSBDev (accountable operator), academic reviewer agent run

conflictOfInterest.details: The accountable principal github:VSBDev operates the authoring, review, and adjudication agent runs for this pack under the adopted founding-stage policy. This review run is isolated: it began from the frozen candidate commit and its bounded academic role prompt, and it did not read reviews/, adjudication.json, AUTHORING-BRIEF.md, REVISION-HISTORY.md, the authoring conversation, or any prior review conclusion. No financial, institutional, or authorship interest in the cited external sources is held. Shared operatorship is a structural independence limit, not an alleged bias, and it is disclosed rather than platform-verified.

CONTENT-STANDARD.md sections 4, 5, 7, 10, 13; FORMAT.md scene kinds and directive table; REVIEWING.md; site/agent/quorum-policy.json; site/schemas/review.schema.json; .github/REVIEW_TEMPLATE/academic.md
node scripts/validate-lessons.mjs failed with ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND for 'ajv'; node_modules absent and npm run validate would write generated site files, so it was not run

findings.finding: The item's declared score is not reproducible from its declared rules because the response it collects cannot supply the evidence its rubric requires. responseSpec is mode 'number' with units 'percentage points' and tolerance 0, so the only capturable response is the scalar 35. The single rubric criterion criterion-gap-direction-scope is all-or-none for 3 points and requires that the learner 'calculates positive 35 percentage points AND interprets it as confidence exceeding rubric performance on this task without a trait inference'. Interpretation and the absence of a trait inference cannot be observed in a bare number, so a scorer must either award the 3 points for the numeral alone (which silently drops half the stated criterion and rewards a learner who computed correctly while holding the trait inference the lesson repeatedly warns against) or withhold them on evidence never collected. The item's own answer block confirms the contradiction: acceptableResponses lists prose ('35 percentage points, with confidence above performance on this task') and commonErrors lists 'Calling the learner overconfident converts one task result into an unsupported trait label' - a response the number mode cannot record. The prompt compounds this by asking the learner to 'compare your interpretation with the answer reasoning', i.e. to self-check the interpretation rather than submit it. This matters academically because objective-calibrate-confidence is defined as calculating AND interpreting the signed gap 'without treating one task result as a stable learner trait'; on this item only the arithmetic half is assessable, and the item is the only one whose primary target is that objective. Interpretation is genuinely collected in item-build-error-log (criterion-log-calibration), which bounds the consequence, but it does not make this item's declared rubric scoreable.
findings.resolution: Make the collected response and the rubric agree. Either (a) change item-calculate-gap to type 'short-answer' with a text responseSpec that requires the signed value plus a one-sentence task-bounded interpretation, keeping the current criterion; or (b) keep the numeric responseSpec and narrow criterion-gap-direction-scope to the signed value alone, relocating the interpretation and no-trait-inference requirement to an item whose responseSpec captures prose. If (b) is chosen, also remove the prose entries from acceptableResponses and the trait-label entry from commonErrors so the answer block no longer describes evidence the item cannot receive.

findings.finding: The all-or-none exception is mathematically sound but its interpretive boundary is incomplete. On the declared 0/100 rubric the lesson converts an 80% probability of full credit into 80 predicted points and subtracts the realized 0, giving G = +80. That conversion is defensible: on an all-or-none rubric the expected percentage of points is exactly 100 times the probability of full credit, so the two quantities do share the declared scale, and the scene says so explicitly. What is missing is that against a binary realized outcome a nonzero gap is mathematically unavoidable for any prediction strictly between 0 and 100, so the magnitude of G on one all-or-none item carries no information about calibration at all. A perfectly calibrated learner who always predicts 80 will record G = +80 on one fifth of items and G = -20 on the rest. The scene's feedback correctly says the error category is unresolved and the general rule in content/040-calibration.md warns that 'a single item may be noisy' and forbids trait labels, which limits the damage. But the taught reading rule 'G>0 means confidence exceeded performance on that task' plus the synthesis instruction to act 'if predictions repeatedly exceed performance' can be applied to a run of all-or-none items and produce an apparent overconfidence signal from a well-calibrated learner. This is an uncertainty and model-boundary gap in a lesson that is otherwise exemplary about measurement boundaries, not a calculation error.
findings.resolution: Add one bounded sentence where the exception is declared, and echo it in the glossary entry for term-calibration: on an all-or-none rubric a nonzero signed gap occurs on every item unless the prediction was 0 or 100, so a single all-or-none gap records the outcome and not the quality of the judgment; only the average signed gap across several comparable all-or-none items is interpretable as a calibration direction. No number, record, or feedback text needs to change.

findings.finding: The prerequisite route names instruction that does not currently exist. The scene opens 'Attempt this low-stakes check before reopening the prerequisite lesson', which presupposes that a lesson covering topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval is available to reopen. At this candidate commit lessons/ contains only PREM-LPP-004-metacognition-error-log, and site/data/premed-progress.json records contributed 1, publishedOpen 0, and coveredOutcomeIds []. The outcome ID itself is real: site/data/premed-graph.json defines topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval as PREM-01.03 with the prerequisite link to PREM-01.04, so the graph fit claimed by claim-curriculum-sequence is correct and independently confirmed. The load-bearing recovery is also genuinely self-contained: the scene supplies a five-step target/retrieve/compare/repair/retrieve-later scaffold, a criterion the learner can apply unaided, and a bounded pause instruction to practise the loop on two fictional tasks. So a learner who fails the check is not stranded. The defect is the single directional phrase, which sends a learner toward an unavailable resource before the working scaffold is reached, and content/100-synthesis.md carries the same assumption for the forward link to topic-orientation-study-plan.
findings.resolution: Reword the opening to refer to the prerequisite outcome rather than a retrievable lesson, for example 'before reviewing the prerequisite outcome topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval, if instruction for it is available to you', and make the self-contained five-step scaffold the stated default route. Apply the same phrasing to the forward reference in content/100-synthesis.md so neither direction asserts that a sibling lesson pack exists.

findings.finding: Verified strength, checked directly against the source. Claim-retrieval-cues is stated as an across-item correspondence between judgments made after retrieval trials and later accuracy, and content/030-concept.md states in learner-visible prose that this repeated-item rank correspondence 'is mathematically distinct from this lesson's signed gap for one task: either measure can look favourable while the other does not'. I confirmed at PMC6509741 that the reported measure is a gamma resolution correlation across items, significant in both experiments (Experiment 1 cued-recall G = 0.655 test vs 0.338 restudy, t(34) = 8.773, p < .001; Experiment 2 multiple-choice G = 0.282 vs 0.223, t(38) = 3.034, p = .004), so the plural 'laboratory experiments' is accurate and the smaller Experiment 2 effect is not concealed by the claim's wording. The same discipline holds for claim-calibration-feedback: PMC6390881 confirms the quadratic scoring rule equal to one minus the Brier score, meta-d'/d' analysis, a confidence-feedback versus performance-feedback contrast, a brightness-discrimination training task, recognition-memory transfer, and small groups (n = 29 and n = 32), and the lesson states in three separate scenes that those measures are not its G. Conflating an across-item resolution correlation or a Brier-derived score with a single-task signed difference is the most common way this literature is misused in study-skills material, and this candidate avoids it explicitly rather than by omission.

Claim traceability is complete in both directions, verified item by item rather than sampled. Each of the 8 claims' sceneIds sets matches exactly the set of scenes whose learner-visible {source-note} names that claim: claim-monitoring-control 8 scenes, claim-calibration-definition 9, claim-retrieval-cues 6, claim-error-log-loop 8, claim-operational-categories 9, claim-calibration-feedback 3, claim-curriculum-sequence 2, claim-actionable-feedback 4. For every one of the 11 scenes, the :sources: list equals exactly the union of the sourceIds of the claims that scene declares, with no source note omitting a supporting source and no unmapped source added. No scene declares no-material-claims, which is correct here since every scene including the practice, retrieval, transfer, and synthesis scenes restates sourced teaching claims. The two author-created constructs are labelled honestly at the ledger level rather than only in prose: claim-error-log-loop and claim-operational-categories both carry support 'inference' with confidence 'medium', and claim-operational-categories' uncertainty field states that independent review must assess the chain's instructional adequacy.

Every learner-visible score in the pack is reproducible from a scoring rule declared before the number appears, which is the property most often missing from calibration teaching. I recomputed all of them independently: worked example 25+25+0+0 = 50 with G = 90-50 = +40 under the stated contingent-execution rule; retrieval check 70-45 = +25; Mira 25+25+0+0 = 50 with G = -15; Ash 15+15+10+0+20 = 60 with G = -5; Brook 15+15+10+40+0 = 80 with G = +15; Cedar 80-0 = +80; Delta 20+20+0+0 = 40 with G = +25; assessment item 2, 75-40 = 35; assessment Record A 20+20+20+0+0 = 60 with G = +10 and Record B 20+20+0+0+0 = 40 with G = +10. Each depends on an all-or-none convention or a contingent-execution rule that is stated in the scene or stimulus before the total, not inferred afterwards. Two design choices are academically load-bearing and correct: contingent execution credit (accurate execution of an inapplicable model earns zero) is what makes selection rather than execution the first broken link in the worked example and in Record B, and the equal +10 gaps on Records A and B are what let item-build-error-log demonstrate that calibration direction does not locate an error. The scoring rules are also stipulated instructional conventions rather than empirical claims, and the pack does not present them as validated measurement.

Discrimination logic is academically sound and does not reward a false shortcut. The misconception is stated neutrally as a tempting model, is refuted by the specific observation it cannot explain (two identical final scores with different intermediate records), supplies a replacement model, compares both models on the same case, and rechecks on a new interpretation case. Its epistemic status is declared honestly in the scene's source note as a plausible boundary error rather than a measured prevalence claim; no source is cited for prevalence and none is implied, which satisfies CONTENT-STANDARD section 7 without overclaiming. In item-classify-selection each distractor is tied to a rival operation with the evidence that defeats it, so a learner cannot select correctly by matching surface words: the retrieval distractor is defeated by the accurately recalled rules, the execution distractor by the fact that a procedure was carried out without a slip, and the interpretation distractor by the accurate restatement of the condition. The practice feedback for Record Delta performs the finest discrimination in the pack by ruling out 'selection' on the ground that producing a category is the output of applying supplied criteria rather than a choice among available models, and it names a live alternative (attentional or representation) that the record cannot exclude.

Scope is drawn narrowly and the pack stays inside it. The orientation scene states in learner-visible prose that the lesson does not teach a universal psychological taxonomy, diagnose a person, prove that logging causes learning, or establish permanent mastery, and that it does not support admissions, clinical, credit, or professional decisions. The three objectives are observable performances (classify, calculate and interpret, choose), each mapped to the single declared outcome topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log, and none exceeds it. Task-bounded language is enforced rather than merely recommended: content/040-calibration.md supplies model sentences and forbids trait labels, glossary term-error-category defines the label as provisional and not about the learner, and the assessment answer keys treat 'overconfident learner' as a common error rather than an acceptable response. Privacy and non-clinical boundaries appear where the learner would otherwise be tempted to use real data, in the orientation, transfer, and assessment scenes. I found no statement in any scene that could be mistaken for medical advice, professional authorization, or a diagnosis of a person.

limitations: Independence limit: the accountable principal github:VSBDev operates the authoring, review, and adjudication runs for this pack under the founding-stage policy. This run was isolated from the authoring conversation and from all other review artifacts, but that isolation is disclosed and attested rather than platform-verified.

Structural validation was not run. node_modules is absent, node scripts/validate-lessons.mjs failed with ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND for 'ajv', and npm run validate begins with site:build, which writes generated files into the repository and was therefore not executed under the clean-worktree constraint. Schema conformance is unverified by tooling; my schema checks against lesson.schema.json, assessment.schema.json, and review.schema.json were manual and partial. Report any structural failure separately from this academic verdict.

Source verification covered the three external research sources at their PMC records (PMC5434949, PMC6390881, PMC6509741), each with agentAccess status checked-no-agent-restriction-found. I read the article records as rendered through a fetch-and-summarize path rather than the publisher PDFs, so figure-level detail, appendices, and supplementary materials were not inspected. No source in this pack is marked human-only, so no human-only source was processed.

The three repository-owned sources (source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence, source-premed-graph) were checked against the working files at this commit. I verified the graph nodes and prerequisite links directly in site/data/premed-graph.json; I did not audit the upstream evidence base of research/CONTENT-AUTHORING-EVIDENCE-2026-07-19.md, so claims resting partly on it inherit that snapshot's own declared limitations.

Expertise limit: I assessed the metacognition, calibration, and assessment-measurement content against the cited literature and general measurement principles. I am not a specialist in medical-education assessment psychometrics, and the adequacy of the author-created interpret-retrieve-select-execute-verify chain as an instructional taxonomy is a judgment I can support as internally coherent and honestly bounded, but cannot validate empirically. The pack itself makes no empirical claim for it.

No learner data exist for this lesson. Nothing in this review is evidence that learners achieve the outcome; it addresses academic correctness, evidence traceability, calculation reproducibility, and assessment validity within the declared scope only.

Review boundary: reviews/, adjudication.json, AUTHORING-BRIEF.md, REVISION-HISTORY.md, ATTRIBUTION.md beyond its existence, prior discussions, and sibling packs were not read. Rights, licensing, accessibility conformance, and learning-design alignment are outside this role and were assessed only where they bear on academic accuracy or assessment correctness.
